results.title: Hyperlink by Nexa AI: On-device AI super assistant for your files | Product Hunt
results.content:
Hyperlink bridges that gap:

 Local-first intelligence: everything runs directly on your device — no data ever leaves.
 Unlimited file context: understands and connects thousands of files and stays in sync.
 Agentic RAG: quality rivaling cloud AI
 No setup, no CLI: always-there floating UI built for anyone.

Try Hyperlink today — see what your own computer already knows about you.

Website: 

Would love to hear what breaks, what surprises you, and what you’d want next. Drop a comment and chat with us on Discord: 

Report

Shake Lyu

remio - Your Personal ChatGPT

remio - Your Personal ChatGPT [...] remio - Your Personal ChatGPT

@alexchen4ai This sounds like a fantastic leap forward for personal knowledge management and privacy! The idea of a local, intelligent AI assistant that truly understands all my files is incredibly appealing. How does Hyperlink handle the computational load of processing "thousands of files" locally, especially on devices with more limited resources?🤔

Report

Alan Zhu

NexaSDK for Mobile

NexaSDK for Mobile

Maker

@alexchen4ai  @lvyanghuang Thanks for your support. Hyperlink is powered by our inference engine called NexaML which provides leading performance for local AI inference. Besides, we did a ton of optimization in the agentic RAG pipeline to further improve the performance.

Enrick Veillette

#### What's great

This product is insane. This is literally what I was hoping to build in my dreams if I had the experience to develop something like that.

The app is so clean and user-friendly. The price is really good for everything they offer, and I love apps that let you use your own API key instead of requiring you to pay ANOTHER monthly fee.

No more copy-pasting into GPT—you work directly within the app you are already using.

#### What needs improvement [...] #### What needs improvement

Honestly, I would say the logo and the branding. I was perplexed when I saw the logo and the name of the app since this is not what I was searching for, but when I watched the video features, I was so impressed. One thing I would also love is maybe a way to input your own icon to make your Fluent more "made for you."

Vadim Ermolin
Fluent

Fluent

Thank you, Enrick! I hope you enjoy Fluent, and stay tuned for some really exciting features coming soon, as well as stability and quality-of-life improvements 😊

results.title: Weavable: Give every AI agent persistent work context | Product Hunt
results.content:
Report

Ishan Roy

What interested me most was the activity graph idea. For this kind of problem, it feels a lot more practical than just relying on RAG. A vector database can store snapshots and context, but it doesn’t really capture what actually caused something to change. Like a Slack message from last week might be the thing that moved a deal forward, but a typical RAG setup only knows the message exists, not the impact it had. Tracking what changed, when it changed, and what triggered it feels much closer to how people actually reason about workflows and decisions. [...] Report

Varun Nair

Weavable

Weavable

Maker

@grantmac\_  @arjun\_reghu Not really. RAG retrieves chunks of unstructured text by semantic similarity and hands them to the model. We do the opposite: deterministic queries against a structured graph that already knows the relationships, the timeline, and what changed.

So the model isn't reasoning over a pile of similar-looking documents. It's reading a current, ranked view of work data with the connections resolved upstream. Different primitive, different failure modes, very different token profile.

Report

Abesh Thakur

Weavable

Weavable

Maker

@grantmac\_ thank you for taking a look, and for the encouragement!

Report

Ishan Roy [...] On the tokens: pulling raw data into the context window costs you twice. Once on ingestion, then again on reasoning, while the LLM connects records, sorts by recency, and figures out what matters. Weavable's graph already knows the relationships and what changed when. The agent queries for the specific signals it needs, and the model only reasons over those.

The 90% is what we see on realistic workflows like pre-meeting briefs, renewal analysis, pipeline summaries, compared to the same thing built on raw MCP calls.

Report

Grant MacLennan

@varunn very cool guys!

Report

Arjun Reghu

@grantmac\_  @varunn So its basically RAG ??

Report

Varun Nair

Weavable

Weavable

Maker

results.title: Agno: Build lightning-fast, multi-modal Reasoning Agents. | Product Hunt
results.content:
Hi everyone!  
  
Sharing Agno, a new open-source library focused on building high-performance, multimodal AI agents. If you're building agentic systems, this looks seriously impressive, especially regarding speed and memory efficiency.  
  
Agno acts as a lightweight framework, providing a unified API for various LLMs and adding capabilities like memory, knowledge stores, tool use, and reasoning.  
  
Key aspects that stand out:  
  
🚀 Lightning Fast & Lightweight: They report huge performance gains over frameworks like LangGraph (claiming 10,000x faster instantiation and 50x less memory on their benchmarks).  
🔌 Model Agnostic: No lock-in! Use models from OpenAI, Anthropic, Cohere, or open-source ones via Ollama, Together, Anyscale, etc. [...] 👁️ Multimodal: Native support for agents working with text, image, audio, and video.  
🤝 Multi-Agent Teams: Built to orchestrate teams of specialized agents.  
🧠 Memory, Knowledge, Tools: Built-in support for memory, vector DBs (for RAG), and adding custom tools.  
📊 Monitoring: Integrates with agno.com for real-time agent monitoring.  
🔓 Open Source (Apache 2.0): Freely available for use and contribution.  
  
For developers building high-performance, multimodal AI agents, Agno offers a powerful and efficient open-source foundation. [...] Elisi : AI-powered Goal Management App

@ansub Agno’s focus on speed, multimodal flexibility, and open-source ethos makes it a tantalizing foundation for developers pushing agentic systems beyond today’s latency-heavy norms. The claims around memory efficiency and multi-agent orchestration could redefine how teams scale AI workflows. But ​​how does Agno handle resource contention in complex, real-world deployments​​—say, when 100+ agents with competing priorities access shared tools or memory? Does the framework prioritize tasks dynamically (e.g., via cost-based optimization), or does it rely on developers to manually define agent hierarchies?

Benjamin Åstrand
Strawberry

Strawberry

This looks cool! Coming to typescript any time soon? :)
